topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam before my eyes, a mocking stain across the meticulously organized pages of the midterm. A C-minus. It felt like a physical blow, knocking the air from my lungs. I stared at the grade, my hand trembling so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. Every single late night fueled by caffeine and sheer panic seemed to have culminated in this hollow, disappointing result. I reread the feedback, the professor’s terse comments echoing in the silence of my cramped dorm room. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat—a mixture of disbelief and utter defeat. I shoved the paper into my backpack, the sound of the zipper a harsh intrusion into the quiet room. I just needed to figure out what to do next, anything, because the weight of expectation had suddenly become unbearable.

[story 2]

My stomach clenched into a tight, cold knot. When I saw the numerical score posted online, the world seemed to tilt. It wasn't just a mark; it was a complete demolition of the plan I’d constructed for the semester. I paced the length of my small apartment three times, my footsteps loud on the linoleum floor, each step a frantic attempt to burn off the sudden, icy rush of anxiety flooding my system. I ran a hand through my already messy hair, feeling the texture of the strands beneath my palm, seeking some anchor in the chaos. "No," I whispered, the sound barely audible. "This isn't right." I slumped onto the edge of my bed, burying my face in my hands, the pressure of the disappointment squeezing the breath from my chest until it was shallow and painful.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library seemed unbearably harsh, illuminating the crumpled paper in my hand. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of shame that burned hotter than any physical exertion. Every calculated study session, every sacrificed social evening, felt utterly worthless now. I looked up at the towering shelves of textbooks, feeling an overwhelming urge to just walk away, to disappear into the stacks. My jaw was tight, my knuckles white where I gripped my pen. I started furiously highlighting sections of the material, not because I believed it would change the grade, but because the repetitive motion was the only thing keeping the churning inside of me from completely unraveling. I needed a fix, a way to reverse this sudden, crushing failure.
